Based on the Novel
When I think of my wife, I always think of her head.
INT. BEDROOM SOMETIME
We see the back of AMY DUNNE'S HEAD, resting on a pillow. NICK (V.0.) I picture cracking her lovely skull, unspooling her brain, Nick runs his fingers into Amy's hair.
Trying to get answers.
He twirls and twirls a lock, a screw tightening.
The primal questions of a marriage: What are you thinking? How are you feeling? What have we done to each other?
AMY wakes,
turns, gives a look of alarm.
EXT. NORTH CARTHAGE MORNING

A carved faux-marble entry--reading FOREST GLEN--ushers us into a ruined HOUSING DEVELOPMENT. Mostly VACANT houses. A few Fourth of July decorations hang in windows. A weird, BUCOLIC air: swaying grasses, stray wildlife.
EXT. NICK DUNNE'S FRONT YARD

NICK DUNNE, 30s, handsome, is taking out the trash; his yard is the only one mowed--all around him WILDERNESS encroaches. The SUN rises over the treeline and blares its FIRST-DEGREE SPOTLIGHT in his face. He looks ILL. He turns and stares back at his HOUSE as if girding himself. He strides across the yard, opens the door. His shadowy figure fills the doorway for a moment. He SHUTS the door.
EXT. BAR PARKING LOT

NICK--wearing noticeably different clothes--arrives under a glaring SUN. Down the street, a troupe of HOMELESS MEN walks single file along the river. Nick's twin sister, GO, 30s, nerdy-hot, is washing mugs. The bar is packed with '80s k+-tsch: board games, toys, posters. Their very own CLUBHOUSE. Ah, the Irish prince graces us with his presence. He sits on the bar's customer side. She flicks suds at him.
Brought you a present.
He sets a decrepit '70s--era Master Mind on the bar.
You loved it. You loved it. Thanks.
She places it behind the bar alongside SORRY!, CLUE, LIFE.
Pour me a bourbon, would ya?
GO glances pointedly at the clock: bourbons. Settles in.
11:09 a.m. She pours two
What's up, Jitters? He shrugs. She tries to wait him out. Fails.
If you don't talk, I'll fill the silence with: an Excruciating Story
He smiles. This is an old, reliable routine.
I do like that one. Or the time I saw a woman who looked exactly like my friend
Monica but it wasn't Monica, it was
--whose name was.. .Monica. Made it kind of interesting.
She gives a look: Talk.
It's a bad day.
Our anniversary. Five. Five?! That came fast.
And furious.

CLOSEUP on a PEN, cursiving across a DIARY. The pen is GIRLY, topped with pink feathers. We see at the top: January 8, 2005. We hear the words as we see them written in pink. AMY (V.0.) I'm so crazy, stupid happy.
INT. BROOKLYN APARTMENT AMY ELLIOTT,
early 30s, gorgeous, is in a crowded hipster party. Dude-heavy. She weaves her way through the guys. AMY

I met a boy. She spots her FRIEND deep-flirting a guy, and stops midway, stuck with TWO BEERS. She makes her way toward a table with picked--over food and scans the room for anyone she knows. She spots NICK DUNNE--he spots her.
A great, gorgeous, sweet, cool-ass guy. Nick weaves his way over. She's setting down her beer.
It's dangerous to set down a monk-- brewed Belgian wheat beer when the party is down to three Beast Lites and a bottle of Pucker.
Might attract some desperate characters.
He gestures toward a group of Williamsburg musician-types: suspenders and broad-brimmed hats.
Things could get ugly. The Amish are on Ruinspringa. They already relieved me of my
artisanal meat platter.
Finally, someone to tell me how to pronounce that word. Meat?
One syllable.
He picks up the beer.
Whose beer am I drinking? (moving closer to her, so they have same POV) What's your type?
They scan the crowd together. Cozy. Nick points to a hornrimmed, haughty DOUCHEBAG.
I can't picture you sitting still
while he bloviates about his postgrad thesis on Proust. Nick points to a sideburned guy in a NOVELTY TEE.
Ironic hipster so self--aware he makes everything a joke? I prefer men who are funny, not
'~funny." Nick points to a wavy-haired granola-yoga type.
Please tell me it's not Deeply Sensitive Emo-Dude
--who says things like "I love
A beat as they scan the room, then face each other.
Corn-fed, salt-of the earth Missouri guy.
Missouri?! How cute.
Ah: native New Yorker!
World ends at the Hudson. I'm l~my.
So tell me, Amy. Who are you?
A. I am an award--winning scrimshander. B. I am a moderately
(taking her hand) A. Your fingers are far too delicate for real scrimshaw work. B. I am a charter subscriber to Middling Warlord Weekly--I'm sure I'd recognize you. So: C. And you? Who are you?
I'm the guy to save you from all this awesomeness.
INT. APARTMENT ELEVATOR
They head down: tipsy, not touching, but thinking about it. So if you write for a men's
In theory, I know what men drink, what men wearAMY How men bullshit.
Not with you.
Amy laughs: ha, ha.
I'm serious.
She stops, studies him.
it's your chin.
My chin?
It's quite villainous. She places a finger over his chin. Tests the view.
No bulishit. 100% truth.
They are huddling together, walking to hail a cab.
I love New York parties because I
get to leave and walk out into New York. The Great What Next? They turn the corner and step into a cloud of powdered sugar as it's funneled into a bakery. A SUGAR SNOWSTORM. Nick grins: Like this!
You know I have to kiss you now.
Is that right?
I would be a fool to let you walk through a sugar storm unkissed.
The sugar floats all around them. A fairytale. They lean in.
Hold on.
Nick brushes her lips clean. They kiss.
INT. NICK'S BEDROOM A SHABBY, CRAMPED garden studio apartment. BARS on the

windows. NICK and AMY are in bed: blissful. Outside, a car's headlights flash: Their sugary prints light up the headboard. Nick Dunne. I really like you. The Master Mind sits untouched as GO sets up LIFE. So is Amy going to do one of her anniversary--whaddaya call it?-- treasure hunts?
You mean the forced march designed to point out what an uncaring, oblivious asshole I am.
Silence. Nick stares at the LIFE board.
LIFE. I don't remember the point.
Deep Hasbro thoughts. Spin. What was the clue last year she got so
(reciting) ~'When your poor Amy has a cold; this dessert just must be sold."
I don't know, Gol
NICK spins, moves his man, lands on Get Married. Few years ago--you'd have known. GO places a pink peg-wife in his car. He glares at it.
Few years ago it was fun. Year One, the traditional gift is paper--so at the end, she gave me a bound notebook.
So I could write my novel. What'd you give her?
A kite. She'd never flown a kite.
Go spins; skips over the Get Married space.
Year Four: flowers. She led me to the dying rosebush in our backyard. The one you never watered. so symbolic. What's the gift for five?
Wood. What'd you get her?
There's no good gift for wood. Go home, fuck her brains out, then smack her with your penis: Some wood for you, bitch!
They laugh. Interrupted by the phone. Go answers.
Bet my gutters need snaking. (picking up phone) Hey, Walter. What's up?.. .Ohl that is weird. OK, thanks. (to Go) Bleecker' s outside. You are way too into that cat.
He's my special furry pal.
He heads to the door, points at the LIFE board.
Tell me how it ends.
EXT. DUNNE HOUSE

NICK pulls up, salutes WALTER, who's on his front porch behind a WALKER. WALTER gives a curt nod. BLEECKER is sitting on the Dunne stairs. Nick scoops him up, heads to the FRONT DOOR, which is GAPING WIDE OPEN. Nick stops in his tracks.
INT. DUNNE HOUSE
NICK doesn't close the door. He sets the cat down. NICK Amy? We follow Nick as he heads up the stairs.
INT. BEDROOM
Neat. Empty. An iron sits on an ironing board, pretty DRESS next to it. No Amy.
INT. HALLWAY UPSTAIRS ROOMS

Nick proceeds--quickly--down the hallway, peering into doors: An OFFICE (his, a disaster); an elaborately pillowed GUEST ROOM, and one room that contains only a LITTER BOX. Nick goes back downstairs, into:
INT. DINING ROOM
Two placemats on the shiny table.
INT. OFFICE
Amy's office. Neat as a pin. Empty.
INT. KITCHEN
Nick runs back through the DINING ROOM, into the:
INT. LIVING ROOM
Nick stops short. The carpet is covered with GLASS SHARDS from the overturned coffee table. END TABLES are SMASHED; an OTTOMAN is UPSIDE DOWN. NICK backs up.
EXT. WALTER'S FRONT PORCH
Walter looks up from his paper as he hears, from across the
Amy!
INT. DUNNE HOUSE
DOOR opens on RHONDA BONEY, 40s, and JIM GILPIN, 20s. Mr. Dunne? I'm Detective Rhonda
Nick walks them in, shows them the scene.
My wife is gone. I came home to this.
They bend down, examine the scene. Hard to read if they're impressed or not. BONEY takes a YELLOW POST-IT and places it on the MANTEL below three upright photo FRAMES.
I'm not someone who hits the panic button, but-It's weird, right? You mind if we look around?
INT. STAIRWELL
They speak in time to the stair steps. How long you two been here?
Two years, September. We used to live in New York.
City?
I was a writer. We were writers. Why'd y'all come back here?
My mom got sick. I'm sorry, how is she?
She's dead.
INT. UPSTAIRS HALLWAY
Boney reaches the landing, eyes Nick like a patient mom. I'm so sorry. They start down the hail.
Now I own The Bar, downtown. With my twin sister, Margo. The Bar! Love the name. Very meta.
INT. BEDROOM
Boney tests the IRON. Hot. Unplugs it. Looks at the dress. Pretty dress. Date night?
BONEY sticks another POST-IT on the IRONING BOARD.
INT. KITCHEN
Nick walks ahead with GILPIN as BONEY lingers: She sees on the baseboard three SPLASHES of rusty RED. Looks more curious than alarmed. A POST-IT down.
INT. AMY'S OFFICE

BONEY enters. Checks out Amy's desk area. BONEY flips through Amy's well-tended desk calendar: "NICK: DENTIST" is set for March 2013. In July 2013 is "BLEECKER: SHOTS." Amy's degrees cover the walls: HARVARD undergrad, Masters in Psych.
One small corner is dedicated to a kids' book series, AMAZING AMY. Photos of Amy, at all ages, with her parents, REND and MARYBETH, in front of posters for the books.
So should I be con(cerned)-- (studying a picture) I remember these books.
CLOSEUP of a dual frame: AMAZING AMY, the iconic cartoon drawing, is grinning from one side. Our real AMY ELLIOTT DUNNE is mimicking the same grin on the other side.
I remember these! Wait. Your wife is Amazing Amy?

CLOSEUP of a DIARY, a PEN--advertising AMAZING AMY--is cursiving across. The eraser topper is a BRIDE with VEIL. The date is February 24, 2007. We see the words as we hear: AMY (V.0.) Amazing Fucking Amy is getting fucking married! That's how the night started.
INT. UPSCALE NEW YORK RESTAURANT TINY book launch party. Posters advertise the AMAZING AMY
book series--all 20. "Written by RAND and MARYBETH ELLIOTT--two psychologists--Who are parents JUST LIKE YOU!"
With me--regular, flawed, Real

Amy--jealous, as always, of the golden child. Perfect, brilliant Amazing Amy. Who is getting fucking married. NICK and Amy are tight together. Waiters are circulating drinks, wearing T--shirts with an impish Amazing Amy and her TRADEMARK line: If it's worth doing, it's worth doing BRIGHT!
Now you can say you came. And in 10
minutes we'll leave.
Perfect, time for a quick tour of
my failings. They walk along the wall of BOOK POSTERS. Stop in front of a poster of: gradeschool AMAZING AMY holding a CELLO. A MUTT beside her.
When I was 10 I quit cello. In the next book, Amazing Amy became a prodigy.
Next POSTER: teen AMAZING AMY playing volleyball.
You don't play volleyball.
I got cut freshman year. ~ made They continue their tour.
And how long did you have a dog? ~ got a dog. Puddles made her more relatable.
They stop in front of the biggest poster: Amazing Amy, in a bridal veil, a BLAND GROOM next to her. The banner reads: 30th Anniversary Special Edition-AMAZING AMY AND THE BIG DAY.

I love your parents, be assholes. but they can In the center of the limp party, RAND and MARYBETH, 60s, cheerily hand out commemorative PENS-identical to the one Amy used for her DIARY. Rand spots them--hands them each a pen.
Painful pause.
Fantastic! Fifteen minutes, tops!
As Rand strides away, Nick gives Amy a look.
This is why I have my trust fund, my Brooklyn brownstone. I can't really complain.
Your parents plagiarized your childhood.
No, they improved upon it, and then peddled it to the masses.
Marybeth pops up, a little tipsy, hugs them.
It's worth doing. .how's that go?
BRIGHT! BRIGHT! The waiters are everywhere in the T-shirts.
Tip of my tongue...

Amy, standing at a cocktail table, deals with a montage of New York media types. NICK hovers nearby. EARNEST GIRL I'm curious whether it's difficult for you to watch Amazing Amy heading down the aisle -and this big party celebrating this fictional weddingNERVOUS INTERN
you are not marriedABOVE-IT-ALL JOURNALIST
Correct. Amazing Amy is always,
always one step ahead of me. Nick cuts in, blocks the journalist.
I have a few questions.
Ah, it's you.
I am here in a strictly journalistic capacity.
He elaborately sets out pad, pen. AMY prepares to be amused.
Amy, you've had the pleasure of dating Nick Dunne for how long?
Is it true that during the course of your relationship, you have performed such gracious gestures as (checking notes) not correcting Nick when he pronounced quinoa as kw+-n--o--a.
An understandable mistake.
He also thought it was a fish. He thinks Velveeta is a cheese.
Touche.
I think it's pronounced tow--chay.
(laughing) You also manage to appear surprised
and delighted when Nick's elderly mother breaks into "New York, New York" every.. .time.. . she sees you.

You also bought Nick his first pair of scissors, correct? And matching stapler. Amy Elliott, you are beyond amazing. You are incredibly smart but entirely unsnobby. You are kind but never a martyr. You surprise me. You challenge me. (MORE)
And--fun fact for our readers--you have a world--class vagina. ~my chokes on her drink.
However my colleagues inform me that as yet you are not married. I am not.
Isn't it time we fixed that? AMY (V.0.) Then the night wasn't so bad anymore.
INT. POLICE STATION BULLPEN DAY
GILPIN is watching NICK from the other side of the glass. NICK's fiddling with his smartphone. HONEY enters. How's our boy?
He's just fucking with his phone. Playing, like, Tetris.
They watch NICK a few more seconds.
Gilpin gives a bored grunt: he couldn't *care less.
Because of those books?
Your wife likes those tabloid crime shows, right?
She's an addict.
OK, so: Beautiful wife, handsome
Wife goes missing on her --turns out she's the star of a book series every woman in the country read as a kid.
Shit. We are the cops of a suburb of a suburb. Let's stay on our toes.
INT. POLICE STATION BULLPEN

BONEY, GILPIN and STEVE the TECH enter. STEVE is brandishing a packet of wrapped Q-tips and his GSR kit. Nick, this is Steve Eckert. He's going to take a swab of your cheek and a hand swipe if that's ok.
This is for?
Gunshot residue, DNA. Just so we can say we did. OK?
OK.
Steve is in and out with no eye contact.
Steve leaves. Nick watches his samples going out the door.

Now, normally, we wouldn't treat this as a missing persons case so quick. We'd tell you to call back in 24 hours. But given the scene in the house and given our spike in violent crime of late, we're going to take this very, very seriously.
OK. Good.
So: We got forensics over at your place. You got somewhere to stay?
My sister's. Good. We're tracking Amy's phone, credit cards. We'll organize searches, put up flyers. We'll hold
a press conference tomorrow.
A press conference?! Want to get the word out, right?
An officer comes in with two styrofoam cups of coffee, slaps a manila envelope on the table. NICK smiles. BONEY frowns.
Sorry. I felt like I was in a Law and Order episode for a second. Bum-- BUM.
You're not, unfortunately.
She stares at NICK, aggressively PONDERING.
No, no, whatever you need.
OK, so you and ~my have been here two years. You tend bar.
I own The Bar. I also teach a
creative-writing class at MVCC.
No kids. So what does ~my do, most days? Woman with all those degrees, what does she do?
She stays busy. Doing what?
Nick attempts a mental inventory. Fails.
She's a big reader.
This lands as lamely as it sounds--and everyone notes it.
housewives, that evening glass of
Just last week: soccer mom, nice lady, got her teeth kicked in over some Oxycontin. Ever since the mall went bust, half the town out of work. . .we can't keep up with the drug problem.
I'm sure that's not it.
Amy got friends we can talk to?
Not really. No. No friends. In this whole town.
She was friendly with my mom... (pause) We've had a problem with the homeless in our neighborhood--
We'll look into it. So you got to The Bar around eleven today. Where were you before then? Just to cross that off.
Well, I was at home until 9 or so. Then I was at Sawyer Beach. Had my coffee, read the paper. You visit with anyone there?
I go there for the quiet.
Nick picks off pieces of his Styrofoam cup; it squeaks.
Is she kinda. .stand-offish?.. .Ivy Nick is visibly uncomfortable. He squeaks at the styrofoam.
Well, she's complicated. She has high expectations.
Boney puts her hand on his to get him to stop the squeaking.
Don't know. You don't know if she has friends, you don't know how she spends her days, you don't know her blood
Sure you guys are married?
Maybe 0? Her folks still in New York?
Yes.
Can they get here in time for the
press conference tomorrow?
I haven't called them yet. You haven't called your wife's parents?
I've been talking with you~ Call them please, Nick. Now.
NICK leaves--BONEY cocks an eyebrow at GILPIN. Door shuts.
Should I know my wife's blood type?
INT. POLICE STATION HALLWAY
Nick on a wall phone, pacing on the short leash of the cord. We hear MARYBETH's TONE on the other end: FEMALE; ANGRY.
I'm so sorry, Marybeth. I just kept hoping she'd walk back in the door.

(a scattering of words) Played.. .tennis.. .on a plane nowNICK We're still not sure what we're dealing with. The cops have been through the house and I've been at the station and we decided. this point they're taking it very seriously. And so I'm calling you.
(a few words) Did they. .know.. if
I'm not positive on that. Have they.
started...
I'm not sure.. .I've been jugglingA detective Rhonda Boney. But. .0K.
B0NEY is standing outside the interrogation room.
My mother-in-law would like to speak with you.
BONEY takes the phone and NICK walks down the hail, chastened, ANGRY. Splashes his face at the water fountain. Breathes. Looks back at BONEY on the phone with Marybeth.
Don't want to be here.
NICK stops in his tracks, listens. Peers into a holding room. BILL DUNNE, 60s, bedraggled, is muttering to himself while a quietly FURIOUS FEMALE officer waits with him.
INT. HOLDING ROOM
I want to go home.
What's going on? This is my dad.
Really? You're Nick Dunne. We've been calling you for hours.
I've been here talking to your detectives. My wife is missing.
The officer begins to softenBILL DUNNE
Your father wandered out of Comfort Hill this morning. We found him walking Route 79. Disoriented. We've been calling.
Nick holds up his phone. No bars.
I have zero reception. But I've been right goddam next door.
Sir, please don't take that tone with me.
Stupid, dumb bitch.
BONEY is revealed to be in the doorway, listening.
You want to drive him home?
EXT. POLICE STATION
BONEY walks NICK to the EXIT. His dad is sitting in the passenger seat of his car. Your dad seems nice. Nick laughs in spite of himself.
He's always been a misogynist asshole. You see him much?
Not since I was 10 and my mom finally divorced his ass.
Boney gives him a pat.
She watches NICK head down the steps. GILPIN joins her.
he says it's not drug related--
Yep. Let's check Sawyer Beach. Two hours there--someone had to have seen him.
Will do. And let's check into our guy here. See what kind of man he is.
He's the kind of man who plays Tetris while his wife is missing.
NICK and his DAD drive SILENTLY through the town. Nick reaches across his DAD and removes from his GLOVEBOX a CHEAPO DISPOSABLE phone. He dials. VOICEMAIL. Hangs up.
INT./EXT. NICK'S CAR

He pulls up front of Comfort Hill Assisted Living. Helps his DAD out and inside. Returns to car in seconds: Fastest dropoff ever. We see a souvenir BIG APPLE pen writing across the DIARY--date at the top: July 5, 2009. We see the words as we hear:
Everyone told us--and told us and
told us--marriage is hard work.
EXT. NEW YORK BOOKSTORE INT. NEW YORK BOOKSTORE
Nick is wearing a backpack, holding an Amy-blue CLUE as he makes his way; Amy follows, all grins.
And compromise.. .and more work. Abandon all hope, ye who enter. Nick is going past Z, past T, past 0, past H.
Nick. With us, two years--it's just good.
I'm not crazy: "When young Amy's hope did wane, she wandered here in search of Jane." Austen right?
They arrive at the A's.
You were an alienated teen. and only Elizabeth Bennet understood
He pulls out Pride and Prejudice. A BLUE ENVELOPE inside. She kisses him. He reads the next clue.
You naughty minx.
She kisses him again, deeply. Looks around. Stacks are empty. Technically we're supposed to fuck at the next stop.
In keeping with tradition. She's already undoing his belt. Hand inside his jeans. We've never fucked in a bookstore.
God bless Jane Austen.

A bookshelf: Packed with books. One tumbles to the floor. Another. Another. Three at once as a hand busts through: Amy trying to get a grip. Through the fallen books we can see Nick and Amy in patchwork: They look like an AD for SEX.
AFTERNOON
Nick and Amy, post-glow, clink glasses and swallow.
I haven't been here since...
I dragged you into the ladies' room on our second date.
Why did we end up here? It was January. We were cold. The light was on.
They both sip.
Books, sex, bourbon. Life is good.
Nick scans under the bar. Finds a blue ENVELOPE. Nick reads the CLUE.
And just got better.
EXT. DIM SUM RESTAURANT INT. DIM SUM RESTAURANT
The table is strewn with dishes, desserts, drinks. A FEAST. The waiter approaches with a gift box, sets it on the table. Year Two, cotton. Nick opens the top, peers in. A strange look on his face.
Because, we had that joke, that our sex was too good for mere--
He pulls out luxurious deep blue sheets. They're out of the wrapping, folded like a newspaper with a bow.
So these are 2,000 thread count--
Nick hands her his BACKPACK, grinning. Amy opens it, pulls out a gift bag--and identical sheets. Nick kisses her.
Sometimes I want to punch us in the face we're so cute.
They kiss again. Stay close.
(whispering) That's crazy though, isn't it?
EXT. GO'S HOUSE INT. GO'S LIVING ROOM
Go is shaking out SHEETS--200 count--while Nick paces. Did they ask if you wanted a lawyer? She tucks the sheets into the couch.
I don't need a lawyer. Did they ask you personal stuff?
About Amy?
They asked why she has no friends here.
What'd you say?
I just said she was complicated. Nick, everyone knows "complicated" is code for bitch.
Nick's phone BUZZES in his pocket. Go is getting bourbon. He hits OFF. She returns with two glasses and a bottle.
that would happen to ?~rny. She always attracts-
Drama. You're with me, Go. You can say it. Just because I don't love Amy doesn't mean I don't care about her. I'm really scared.
Go tosses a pillow at Nick, waits for a reaction. Nothing.
EXT. DUNNE HOUSE

BONEY is drinking a huge COFFEE as she and GILPIN walk toward the house. We can see a half block away, NOELLE HAWTHORNE, PREGNANT, towing identically dressed toddler TRIPLETS. Detective, detective Noelle catches up. An air of annoying self-importance. In the background, we can see cops scouring the neighborhood.
I'm Noelle Hawthorne. I'm Amy's best friend.
Boney and Gilpin exchange a look: A friend?!
Do you know anything yet? I'm so sorry, I got guys on the clock. Give me 30 minutes? That's usually bathtime. We'll talk between shampoos.
INT. DUNNE LIVING ROOM
A half dozen officers are on scene: photos, fingerprints. OFFICERS ARE BAGGING ALL THE TRASH. BONEY, GILPIN and DONNELLY, forensics, hit the stairs.
Luminol sweep.
INT. BEDROOM
As they reach the bedroom, we can see an officer next door in Nick's OFFICE, SIFTING through the contents of the FIREPLACE and BAGGING. A uniformed officer awaits them in the BEDROOM.
House is rented, in her name. Car
Phones, credit cards, utilities all in her name. Even his bar is in her name.
I don't know that's so surprising.
No, but it is humiliating. (motioning to closet) It's way in the back there.

BONEY heads into the walk-in, dresses and shirts swaying as she passes. At the far back is Amy's dresser. A drawer is open. Inside is a BLUE ENVELOPE marked CLUE ONE. BONEY exits the closet displaying it for GILPIN. We have our First Clue! She rips open the envelope.
INT. GO'S LIVING ROOM MORNING

NICK is asleep on the couch, an empty bottle of bourbon next to him. GO nudges him awake, proffering a Coke and Advil. Nick takes them silently, guzzles the Coke, checks his watch. TITLE CARD: July 6, 2012
I should shower. Go just like that. You've been up all night. You want to look like you've been up all night. (pause) Be careful today, OK?
That's a weird thing to say. When you're upset, you bottle up. You can seem.
angry.
Please don't say like Dad. Or else you swing into your Mama's
boy charm offensive--it can feel glib.
Great, I'll try to balance on the exact edge of your emotional razor. Fucking press conference.
Myself? Silence. Because the obvious answer is no.

NICK and GO arrive at the police station and get out of car. BONEY walks NICK and GO in. The busy crowd of cops parts: we see RAND and MARYBETH, SUITCASES at their feet. Your in-laws made it. NICK approaches warily. RAND hugs him fiercely. MARYBETH stays outside the circle.
We played tennis last night, Nick! I just can't get over it.
Marybeth, I'm sorry. (to Marybeth) We're here now.
I knew you shouldn't have moved back here.
(pointed) We didn't have a lot of choice. We are all worried, we are all scared. But we are all here now, and we will find Amy. Together.

He pulls MARYBETH in on the hug. She allows it but keeps tracking EVERYTHING in the office. Boney and Go POV on group hug. They get along? Honestly, here's the secret to Nick. He looks like the preppy asshole from the ~80s teen movie, but he's really the A/V nerd with the pet ferret.
INT. STATION CONFERENCE ROOM

BONEY leads the ELLIOTTS and NICK into a room of a HALF DOZEN BORED LOCAL REPORTERS: Texting, eating, gabbing. As they catch sight of handsome NICK, they put down their sandwiches. AMY's gorgeous MISSING poster is revealed. An AMAZING AMY book jacket is revealed. Everyone is extremely attentive.
